papers.title: Ailanthone as a novel AKT1 inhibitor that induces mitochondrial apoptosis and exerts anti-tumor activity in cervical cancer.
papers.abstract: As a significant global burden on women's health, cervical cancer necessitates the exploration of novel therapeutic strategies driven by the limitations inherent in the current treatment options. Ailanthone (AIL), a natural compound derived from Ailanthus altissima, has significant inhibitory effects against various tumor types. However, its function in cervical cancer and associated molecular mechanisms are yet to be elucidated. This study aims to explore the regulatory effect of AIL on cervical cancer and clarify its potential value in anti-tumor therapy.
papers.summary: This study identifies ailanthone, a natural compound from Ailanthus altissima, as a novel inhibitor of AKT1 that induces mitochondrial apoptosis in cervical cancer cells. The findings suggest ailanthone has potential as a therapeutic agent for cervical cancer by targeting the AKT1 signaling pathway.

papers.title: Exploration and experimental validation of oxidative stress-related diagnostic genes in interstitial cystitis based on transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis (IC) is a chronic pain syndrome with an elusive diagnosis and poorly understood pathogenesis, in which oxidative stress (OS) is increasingly implicated. This study aimed to identify and validate OS-related diagnostic biomarkers for IC using an integrative computational and experimental approach.
papers.summary: This study identifies and validates oxidative stress-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis, a chronic pain condition with unclear causes. Using transcriptomic data and experimental validation, the authors pinpoint specific genes that could aid in diagnosis and understanding of the disease.

papers.title: Monodisperse PEG engineering for quantifiable surface conjugation on PLGA nanoparticles.
papers.abstract: Surface PEGylation is a well-established strategy to enhance the stability, functionality, and bioavailability of nanoparticles (NPs) in biomedical applications. However, the widespread use of polydisperse PEG derivatives limits the ability to precisely quantify surface PEGylation efficiency (mol%) and evaluate its effect on NP properties. In this study, we present a systematic approach to engineer PLGA NPs using structurally defined monodisperse PEG-diamine derivatives of varying chain lengths (PEG6, PEG26, PEG45), synthesized and purified by chromatographic methods and covalently attached vi
papers.summary: This paper presents a method to engineer PLGA nanoparticles with precisely defined monodisperse PEG chains, enabling accurate quantification of surface PEGylation efficiency. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional polydisperse PEG, allowing better control over nanoparticle properties for biomedical applications.

papers.title: Enterovirus RNase L inhibiting RNAs are highly conserved with limited phylogenetic distribution.
papers.abstract: RNA molecules form specific 3D structures that facilitate a variety of functions through interactions with other macromolecules. Many RNA viral genomes maintain these structures to interact with and evade host immunity machinery. One such element, the competitive inhibitor RNA (ciRNA), discovered in the protein coding region of the poliovirus serotype 1 (PV1) genome, inhibits a host antiviral protein, ribonuclease L (RNase L). Although some functionally essential structural motifs of the PV1 ciRNA have been studied, the extent of its evolutionary conservation and other structural requirements
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study investigates the evolutionary conservation of competitive inhibitor RNAs (ciRNAs) in enteroviruses, which are RNA structures that inhibit the host antiviral protein RNase L. The authors find that these ciRNA motifs are highly conserved across enteroviruses but have a limited phylogenetic distribution, suggesting they play a critical role in immune evasion for specific viral lineages. The work provides insights into the structural requirements and evolutionary constraints of these host-pathogen interaction elements.

papers.title: Pep2Mol: 3D Molecule Generation Targeting Protein-Protein Interfaces with Diffusion Models.
papers.abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are central to biological processes. Designing small molecules that modulate dysregulated PPIs holds strong promise for targeting undruggable proteins. However, existing structure-based drug design approaches focus on well-defined small-molecule binding pockets and struggle to generalize to large, shallow, and chemically complex PPI interfaces. Here, we introduce Pep2Mol, a diffusion-based generative model for 3D molecule design that targets orthosteric PPI sites by explicitly incorporating binding peptides or proteins as structural guidance, moving beyond c
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: Pep2Mol is a new AI model that uses diffusion to generate 3D small molecules designed to target protein-protein interaction (PPI) interfaces, which are typically difficult to drug. By incorporating the structure of binding peptides or proteins as guidance, it overcomes the limitations of traditional drug design methods that struggle with the shallow, complex surfaces of PPI sites. This approach offers a promising strategy for developing drugs against previously 'undruggable' proteins involved in key biological processes.

papers.title: Pratenol B, Eriodictyol, Losbanine, and Isookanin, as potential EGFR and HRAS inhibitors in oral squamous cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the most prevalent form of head and neck cancer and remains highly prevalent in developing countries. Disease management is challenged by the increasing prevalence of risk factors, limited treatment modalities, adverse side effects, and growing drug resistance, highlighting the need for safer and more effective treatment. This study investigates the potential of phytochemicals as targeted inhibitors of key dysregulated biomarkers in OSCC patients. RNA sequencing and pathway analysis identified the MAPK signaling pathway as the most significantly altered,
papers.summary: This study identifies four phytochemicals (Pratenol B, Eriodictyol, Losbanine, and Isookanin) as potential inhibitors of EGFR and HRAS proteins in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC). Using RNA sequencing and pathway analysis, the researchers found the MAPK signaling pathway to be most altered in OSCC, suggesting these natural compounds could offer a safer treatment alternative by targeting key dysregulated biomarkers.

papers.title: In silico design and validation of high-affinity RNA aptamers for SARS-CoV-2 comparable to neutralizing antibodies.
papers.abstract: Nucleic acid aptamers hold promise for clinical applications, yet understanding their molecular binding mechanisms to target proteins, and efficiently optimizing their binding affinities, remain challenging. Here, we present CAAMO (Computer-Aided Aptamer Modeling and Optimization), which integrates in silico aptamer design with experimental validation to accelerate the development of aptamer-based RNA therapeutics. Starting from the sequence information of a reported RNA aptamer, Ta, for the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, our CAAMO method first determines its binding mode with the spike protein's r
papers.summary: This study presents CAAMO, a computational method that integrates in silico aptamer design with experimental validation to rapidly optimize RNA aptamers for the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, achieving binding affinities comparable to neutralizing antibodies. The approach successfully improved a known aptamer's binding affinity, demonstrating a powerful pipeline for developing high-affinity RNA therapeutics.

papers.title: Structural studies and functional engineering of NanX: an anhydro-sialic acid transporter from Escherichia coli.
papers.abstract: NanT and NanX are bacterial transporters that import the sialic acids, N-acetylneuraminate and 2,7-anhydro-n-acetylneuraminate, respectively. Here, we used complementary biophysical and computational approaches to structurally characterise Escherichia coli NanX. Size exclusion chromatography, analytical ultracentrifugation and low-resolution cryo-electron microscopy reveal that NanX exists in both monomeric and dimeric states following purification. Molecular modelling and substrate docking identify key residues likely involved in 2,7-anhydro-n-acetylneuraminate recognition. Using this informa
papers.summary: This study characterizes the structure of NanX, a bacterial transporter for an unusual sialic acid, using biophysical methods and computational modeling. The protein exists as both a monomer and dimer, and key residues for substrate recognition were identified, which could guide future engineering efforts.

papers.title: Structural insights into multitargeting Mycobacterium tuberculosis Pkn kinases.
papers.abstract: Tuberculosis remains one of the leading major global health challenges, driven by the emergence of multidrug-resistant and extensively drug-resistant bacterial strains. Resistant strains complicate treatment, which often requires prolonged use of toxic second- and third-line drugs. Protein phosphorylation plays critical roles in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, with serine/threonine kinases PknA, PknB, and PknG being essential for survival, virulence, and persistence. In this study, we screened an in-house kinase inhibitor library to identify compounds targeting these kinases. Four structurally div
papers.summary: This study identifies four compounds from a kinase inhibitor library that target three essential serine/threonine kinases (PknA, PknB, PknG) in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, providing structural insights into multitargeting strategies against tuberculosis. The findings offer a potential avenue for developing new drugs to combat multidrug-resistant and extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis strains.

papers.title: Thioredoxin Reductase as a Target for Antibacterial Gold Compounds in Burkholderia cenocepacia: Disclosing the Molecular Basis of Enzyme Inhibition.
papers.abstract: Thioredoxin reductase from Burkholderia cenocepacia (Bc-TrxR) is a recognized intracellular target of antibacterial gold(I) compounds, yet the molecular basis of enzyme inhibition remains unclear. Here, we report an integrated structural, biophysical, and biochemical investigation of recombinant Bc-TrxR and its interaction with three prototypical gold(I) agents: auranofin (AF) and two trimethylphosphine-thiolate derivatives (Au1 and Au2), previously identified as potent enzyme inhibitors. The enzyme was expressed, purified, and its crystal structure solved at 2.52 Å resolution, revealing a hom
papers.journal: Chembiochem : a European journal of chemical biology
papers.summary: This study reveals how gold-based antibacterial compounds inhibit thioredoxin reductase in Burkholderia cenocepacia, a pathogen causing lung infections in cystic fibrosis patients. By solving the enzyme's crystal structure and analyzing its interactions with three gold compounds, the researchers provide molecular-level insights that could guide development of new antibiotics against this drug-resistant bacterium.

papers.title: Discovery of novel anti-Toxoplasma gondii agent derived from thiazolidinone-isatin hybrids: synthesis, biological assessment, and computational modeling.
papers.abstract: Toxoplasmosis is a globally prevalent parasitic infection that disproportionately impacts immunocompromised individuals and remains a significant public health challenge. The limitations of current standard-of-care therapies, marked by severe adverse effects and an inability to eradicate persistent cysts, necessitate the urgent development of novel, safer, and more efficacious anti-Toxoplasma agents. Employing a rational molecular hybridization strategy, we designed and synthesized a novel series of seventeen thiazolidinone-isatin hybrid derivatives to evaluate their in vitro inhibitory effect
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized a new series of thiazolidinone-isatin hybrid compounds as potential treatments for toxoplasmosis, a parasitic infection that is particularly dangerous for immunocompromised individuals. Several of the synthesized compounds showed promising anti-Toxoplasma activity in laboratory tests, offering a potential starting point for developing safer and more effective therapies than current treatments.

papers.title: Discovery of novel METTL3 inhibitors: design, synthesis, and in silico and in vitro evaluations.
papers.abstract: Methyltransferase-like protein 3 (METTL3) is a promising therapeutic target for Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML). To discover novel METTL3 inhibitors, we designed a series of compounds by hybridizing the natural product Genistein and the non-nucleoside METTL3 inhibitor STM2457. Among the 31 de novo-generated compounds subjected to molecular docking, the top-scoring hit H1 was selected for chemical synthesis and its structure was confirmed by NMR and MS. H1 showed an inhibitory activity against METTL3 with an IC50 value of 980.3 nM, significantly better than Genistein (37.31% inhibition at 40 µM).
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized a new compound, H1, that inhibits the METTL3 protein, a promising target for treating Acute Myeloid Leukemia. H1 showed much stronger inhibitory activity than the natural product Genistein, with an IC50 of 980.3 nM. This work provides a lead compound for further development of METTL3-targeted therapies.

papers.title: Phytochemical profiling, network pharmacology, and molecular dynamics simulations of Sinularia levi metabolites as potential α-glucosidase inhibitors in metabolic syndrome.
papers.abstract: This study has investigated the phytochemical composition and therapeutic potential of the Red Sea soft coral Sinularia levi. Five compounds (1-5) comprising three glyceryl derivatives, a ceramide, and a diterpene were isolated and characterized. To elucidate their role in metabolic syndrome, an integrated network pharmacology and structure-based modeling workflow was employed. This approach identified 45 shared targets between S. levi metabolites and disease proteins, clustered into inflammatory and metabolic nodes, with α-glucosidase (GAA) emerging as a primary target. Molecular docking agai
papers.summary: This study identifies compounds from the Red Sea soft coral Sinularia levi that may inhibit α-glucosidase, a key target in metabolic syndrome. Using network pharmacology and molecular dynamics simulations, the authors found that five isolated compounds, particularly a diterpene, show strong binding to α-glucosidase, suggesting potential as natural therapeutic agents.

papers.title: Insights into the toxic effects of moxifloxacin in Allium cepa through multiparametric experimental and in silico analyses.
papers.abstract: In this study, the cytogenetic and biochemical effects of moxifloxacin (MOX), a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic, on Allium cepa L., a non-target plant model, were investigated using a combination of experimental and in silico approaches. Onion roots were exposed to MOX at concentrations of 15, 29, and 57 µg/L. Genotoxicity was assessed using the mitotic index (MI), micronucleus (MN), chromosomal aberrations (CAs), and the Comet assay. The findings showed that MOX reduced mitotic activity in a dose-dependent manner (by 21% in the highest dose group) and significantly increased the
papers.summary: This study shows that the antibiotic moxifloxacin (MOX) causes genetic damage and biochemical stress in onion plants, a non-target organism. MOX reduced cell division and increased DNA damage in a dose-dependent manner, suggesting potential ecological risks from antibiotic pollution.

papers.title: Cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin.
papers.abstract: Fibronectin (FN) and transglutaminase 2 (TG2) engage in a high-affinity interaction. This interaction affects anchoring, migration and survival of cells, and high cellular expression of TG2 is associated with metastasis potential of cancers. Previous work has mapped the interaction to involve the N-terminal part of TG2 and an N-terminally located 45 kDa domain of FN (FN45). In particular, the I8 module of FN45 is reported to be necessary and sufficient for TG2 binding. Here, we present the cryo-EM structure of a complex between human TG2 and FN45 at 2.8 Å resolution. In the structure, the dist
papers.summary: This study presents the first high-resolution cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 (TG2) and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin (FN45), revealing the molecular details of their interaction. The structure shows how the I8 module of FN45 binds to the N-terminal part of TG2, providing insights into how this interaction influences cell adhesion, migration, and cancer metastasis.

papers.title: Rational engineering of the Arabidopsis thaliana plant cysteine oxidase 4 active site can reduce biochemical activity and improve submergence tolerance.
papers.abstract: The increase in flooding events due to climate change is having devastating effects on food security. Plants can respond to submergence by reducing their energy requirements and switching their metabolism from oxidative phosphorylation to fermentation. This response is promoted by group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs) whose stability is dependent on oxygen availability; in normoxia, plant cysteine oxidase (PCO) enzymes catalyze oxidation of ERF-VII N-terminal Cys residues to trigger ERF-VII degradation via the Cys/Arg-N-degron pathway. In hypoxia, reduced PCO activity prevents ERF-VII
papers.summary: This study shows that rationally engineering the active site of the Arabidopsis cysteine oxidase PCO4 can reduce its biochemical activity, which in turn improves plant tolerance to submergence. By slowing the oxygen-dependent degradation of key transcription factors (ERF-VIIs), the engineered enzyme helps plants better survive flooding conditions. This work provides a potential strategy for developing flood-tolerant crops through targeted enzyme modification.

papers.title: Profiling fungal secondary metabolites with anti-leishmanial activity: an integrated in silico and in vitro study.
papers.abstract: Numerous reports of drug resistance in Leishmania major, the causative agent of cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL), underscore the need to discover novel therapeutics. Fungal secondary metabolites (SMs) are valuable resources for drug development and can serve as antimicrobial agents, enzyme inhibitors, and lead compounds for novel medicines. A three-dimensional model of the L. major sterol 14α-demethylase (LmCYP51) enzyme was built using L. infantum CYP51 as a template. A library of 1,167 compounds was virtually screened against LmCYP51 using high-throughput docking. Eleven promising candidates wer
papers.summary: This study used computational modeling and virtual screening to identify fungal secondary metabolites that could inhibit a key enzyme in Leishmania major, the parasite causing cutaneous leishmaniasis. Eleven promising compounds were selected for further testing, offering a potential new avenue for drug development against drug-resistant leishmaniasis.

papers.title: Mechanistic and structural elucidation of π-π end-stacking interactions of a novel SO2NH-anthraquinone derivative with telomeric G-quadruplex d-[TTAGGGT]4: integrated NMR, spectroscopic, electrochemical, and computational evidence.
papers.abstract: Targeting telomeric G-quadruplex (G4) DNA has emerged as a promising anticancer strategy by inhibiting telomerase activity and disrupting telomere maintenance. In the present study, we report a comprehensive structural, spectroscopic, electrochemical, and computational investigation of a novel anthracene-9,10-dione derivative, N-(9,10-dioxo-9,10-dihydroanthracen-1-yl)-3-methyl-2-(phenylsulfonamido)butanamide (1-VAQ), and its interaction with the parallel G-quadruplex d-[TTAGGGT]4. To evaluate binding selectivity, comparative studies were also performed using duplex DNA as a control. 1H NMR spe
papers.summary: This study investigates how a novel anthraquinone derivative binds to telomeric G-quadruplex DNA, a structure implicated in cancer cell immortality. Using a combination of NMR, spectroscopy, electrochemistry, and computational modeling, the authors elucidate the specific π-π end-stacking interactions that stabilize the complex. The work provides mechanistic insights that could inform the design of new anticancer agents targeting telomeres.

papers.title: In silico discovery and interaction analysis of a Datura stramonium defensin-like peptide targeting cancer-related receptors.
papers.abstract: Cancer continues to be a major global health burden, with receptor tyrosine kinases such as EGFR, ERBB2, and VEGFR-3 being critical therapeutic targets due to their central roles in tumor growth, survival, and angiogenesis. Current therapies, while effective in some contexts, face limitations including resistance, toxicity, and high cost, highlighting the need for novel multi-target approaches. In this study, we report the isolation and computational characterization of a novel defensin-like peptide (DEFL) from Datura stramonium (GenBank accession KT371458). The peptide sequence encoded 74 ami
papers.summary: Researchers identified a new defensin-like peptide from the Datura stramonium plant and used computational methods to predict its potential to bind multiple cancer-related receptors, including EGFR, ERBB2, and VEGFR-3. This in silico discovery suggests the peptide could serve as a multi-target therapeutic candidate, offering a novel approach to overcome limitations of current cancer therapies.

papers.title: Designing a chimeric multi-epitope vaccine against Candida auris using reverse vaccinology approach targeting the agglutinin-like protein N-terminal domain.
papers.abstract: Candida auris is an emerging multidrug resistant fungal pathogen associated with high mortality rates, rapid global dissemination and resistance to conventional antifungal therapies. It's remarkable ability to evade host immune responses and persist in health care setting demands the development of effective immunotherapeutic strategies. In this study, a reverse vaccinology and immunoinformatics based approach was employed to design a novel chimeric multi-epitope vaccine targeting surface expose N-terminal domain of the agglutinin like protein involved in host pathogen interactions. High affin
papers.summary: Researchers used computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine against the drug-resistant fungus Candida auris by targeting a key surface protein. The vaccine candidate showed high binding affinity in simulations, suggesting it could stimulate a protective immune response. This approach offers a potential strategy for developing immunotherapies against this emerging pathogen.

papers.title: Novel imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-based α-glucosidase inhibitors: synthesis, biological evaluations, and computational studies.
papers.abstract: In an attempt to identify novel α‑glucosidase inhibitors, a new series of substituted imidazo[1,2‑a]pyridine derivatives (10a-10ab) was designed, synthesized, and evaluated for their inhibitory activities. All compounds exhibited potent inhibition, with IC50 values ranging from 12.01 to 93.01 µM, significantly better than acarbose IC50 = 750.02 µM). SAR analysis highlighted the critical role of para‑substitution on the benzamide ring, especially methoxy groups. The most potent compound, 10s (IC50 = 12.01 µM), acted as a competitive inhibitor (Ki = 21 µM) with no α‑amylase inhibition, indicatin
papers.summary: Researchers designed and tested new imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine compounds as α-glucosidase inhibitors, with the best compound (10s) showing 62-fold stronger inhibition than the standard drug acarbose. The study identified key structural features for activity and demonstrated that these compounds are selective for α-glucosidase over α-amylase, suggesting potential for safer diabetes treatments.

papers.title: Targeting GALNT7 Disrupts the TAZ O-GalNAcylation Feedback Loop to Suppress Gallbladder Cancer Progression.
papers.abstract: Gallbladder cancer (GBC) is a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options and dismal prognosis. Identifying the factors driving GBC progression is crucial for developing potent preventive and therapeutic approaches. Here, using quantitative proteomics, we identify GALNT7 as the most significantly upregulated glycosyltransferase in GBC tissues, associated with adverse clinical outcomes. Mechanistically, GALNT7 physically interacts with TAZ and catalyzes O-GalNAcylation at Ser307, thereby inhibiting K48-linked ubiquitination through the recruitment of the deubiquitinase USP7 and stabilizi
papers.summary: This study identifies the glycosyltransferase GALNT7 as a key driver of gallbladder cancer progression. Using quantitative proteomics, the authors show that GALNT7 modifies the TAZ protein via O-GalNAcylation, which stabilizes TAZ and promotes tumor growth, suggesting a potential therapeutic target.

papers.title: Melatonin reprograms antioxidant defenses to suppress ferroptosis via Homer1a/mGluR1-Nrf2/xCT signaling after retinal ischemia-reperfusion.
papers.abstract: Melatonin (Mel) exerts antioxidant and anti-ferroptosis effects not only via the canonical receptors Mt1/Mt2 but also via metabotropic glutamate receptor 1 (mGluR1)/glutamate-mediated signaling. Nevertheless, the regulatory mechanism by which Mel modulates mGluR1/glutamate signaling in retinal ischemia-reperfusion (I/R) injury and its downstream effects on ferroptosis and oxidative stress remain poorly understood. Here, bulk RNA sequencing identified that the Mel receptors Mt1/Mt2 and ferroptosis/iron-redox regulators (Gpx4, Fth1, and xCT) were significantly downregulated after retinal I/R. Me
papers.summary: This study reveals that melatonin protects retinal cells from ischemia-reperfusion injury by activating the Homer1a/mGluR1-Nrf2/xCT signaling pathway, which reprograms antioxidant defenses and suppresses ferroptosis. The findings provide a new mechanism for melatonin's neuroprotective effects beyond its canonical receptors, offering potential therapeutic targets for retinal diseases involving oxidative stress and iron-dependent cell death.

papers.title: Mechano-reprogramming of tumor-associated macrophages by engineering nanogel stiffness for suppression of tumor development.
papers.abstract: Nanoparticle mechanical properties, as critical factors in tumor targeting drug delivery, have recently been revealed to regulate cellular biological functions. While macroscopic mechanical stimuli are demonstrated to reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), it is unclear how TAMs respond to microscopic stimuli from nanoparticles. Herein, we demonstrated that the stiff 15%NGs presented stronger interactions with integrin than the soft 2%NGs, promoting the polymerization of actin and the activation of yes-associated protein 1. Therefore, the pro-inflammatory phenotype of TAMs was enhanced
papers.summary: This study shows that the stiffness of nanogels can reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) from a pro-tumor to a pro-inflammatory state. Stiffer nanogels interact more strongly with integrin receptors on TAMs, activating actin polymerization and YAP1 signaling, which enhances anti-tumor immune responses. This mechano-reprogramming approach offers a potential strategy for cancer immunotherapy by engineering nanoparticle mechanical properties.

papers.title: Subtype-selective binding of milk β-casomorphins across the opioid receptor family revealed by molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation.
papers.abstract: β-casomorphins (BCMs) are opioid-like peptides produced during the digestion of β-casein in the gastrointestinal tract. They are also involved in other gastrointestinal and neurophysiological processes. Although the effect of BCM7 on the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) has been extensively studied, the molecular interaction mechanisms of other BCMs with different opioid receptors have not been elucidated. This study aimed to investigate the receptor-specific binding behavior of BCM3-BCM11 across the μ-, δ-, κ-, and nociceptin/orphanin FQ peptide (NOP) opioid receptors using structure-based computation
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to investigate how different milk-derived opioid peptides (β-casomorphins) bind to various opioid receptors, revealing subtype-specific interactions beyond the well-studied μ-opioid receptor. The findings provide molecular insights into how these dietary peptides may exert diverse physiological effects through different receptor pathways.

papers.title: Design and Engineering of an Artificial Bifunctional N-Deacetylase/N-Sulfotransferase for the Biosynthesis of N-Sulfated Heparosan.
papers.abstract: Heparin is a widely used anticoagulant, while its traditional animal-derived production faces significant challenges in safety and scalability. The active expression of the initiating-step bifunctional enzyme N-deacetylase/N-sulfotransferase (NDST) in bacteria remains a key bottleneck in heparin biosynthesis. Here, we firstly mined and characterized diverse polysaccharide N-deacetylases (NDases) in Escherichia coli and revealed several NDases that are active toward the deacetylation of heparosan. An artificial NDDr-RL-ST was designed and constructed by fusing an active NDase with a heterogenou
papers.summary: Researchers engineered an artificial bifunctional enzyme (NDDr-RL-ST) by fusing a bacterial N-deacetylase with a heterologous sulfotransferase, enabling efficient biosynthesis of N-sulfated heparosan in E. coli. This overcomes a key bottleneck in heparin production, offering a safer and scalable alternative to animal-derived heparin.

papers.title: Host-dependent optimization of rabies virus-like particle making in insect cells using a low-level control system.
papers.abstract: Rabies causes over 59,000 deaths annually, highlighting the need for scalable and cost-effective vaccines. Virus-like particles (VLP) are promising candidates, but their production depends on host physiology and process parameters. Here, rabies VLP were produced in Spodoptera frugiperda (Sf9) and High Five insect cells, employing a three-level full factorial design to optimize the impact of the expression system, time of infection (TOI), and harvest time (HT). The cell lines exhibited complementary expression profiles: Sf9 achieved the highest rabies virus glycoprotein (RVGP) expression, reach
papers.summary: This study optimizes the production of rabies virus-like particles (VLPs) in two insect cell lines (Sf9 and High Five) using a low-level control system. By testing different expression systems, infection times, and harvest times, the researchers found that Sf9 cells produced the highest levels of the rabies glycoprotein, which is key for vaccine development. The work aims to make rabies vaccines more scalable and cost-effective.

papers.title: Aromatic π-stacking stabilizes an α-helical SARS-CoV-2 MPER peptide that mimics the post-fusion spike and enables potent antiviral activity.
papers.abstract: Membrane fusion between SARS-CoV-2 and host cells is mediated by the spike protein and involves the membrane-proximal external region (MPER), a tryptophan-rich sequence implicated in viral entry. Here, we investigated the feasibility of MPER-derived peptides as potential antiviral agents and examined the structural determinants underlying their activity. A series of truncated MPER peptides was synthesized and evaluated for antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2 in cellular assays, revealing potent inhibitory potency against different variants, with nanomolar IC50 values associated with sequence
papers.summary: This study shows that short peptides derived from the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein's membrane-proximal external region (MPER) can potently inhibit viral entry, with nanomolar activity against multiple variants. The key structural feature enabling this activity is aromatic π-stacking that stabilizes an α-helical conformation mimicking the post-fusion spike state.

papers.title: Theoretical study on N(sp2)+-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds in the context of drug discovery.
papers.abstract: C-H···O hydrogen bonds are regarded as weak and overlooked in structure-based drug design. Here, we demonstrate through quantum-chemical calculations that N(sp2)⁺-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions exhibit strong and directional interactions with carbonyl oxygen atoms. Density-functional-theory (DFT) and natural-bond-orbital (NBO) analyses indicated that cationization increases the positive charge on adjacent sp2 C-H protons, thereby enhancing their proton-donating ability. Fragment-based symmetry-adapted-perturbation-theory (F-SAPT) analyses revealed that these interactions
papers.summary: This study uses quantum-chemical calculations to show that N(sp2)⁺-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions are strong and directional, contrary to the common view that C-H···O bonds are weak. The findings suggest these interactions could be useful in structure-based drug design by improving ligand binding.

papers.title: A structure-based epitope tagging approach identifies vulnerable sites on the malarial P36-P52 protein complex for antibody-mediated neutralization of Plasmodium sporozoites.
papers.abstract: Malaria is caused by apicomplexan parasites of the genus Plasmodium, which are transmitted through the bite of Anopheles mosquitoes that inject sporozoites (SPZs) into the skin. SPZs migrate to and infect the liver for an initial round of replication. SPZs and liver stages have long been considered as ideal targets for malaria vaccines. The main SPZ surface protein, the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), is the target of currently approved malaria vaccines and prophylactic antibody therapies. Studies in rodent malaria models have shown that anti-CSP antibodies exert their protective effect mainly
papers.summary: This study identifies vulnerable sites on the P36-P52 protein complex of malaria sporozoites using a structure-based epitope tagging approach, revealing targets for antibody-mediated neutralization. The findings could inform the development of new malaria vaccines or antibody therapies that block sporozoite infection of the liver.

papers.title: Structure of the Beat-Side complex reveals their interaction is required for neuromuscular system wiring.
papers.abstract: Nervous system assembly is guided by the actions of cell surface receptors. In Drosophila, members of the beaten path (Beat) and sidestep (Side) protein families have been described as axon guidance receptor-cue pairs, in addition to roles in specifying synaptic connectivity in the optic lobe. To understand the molecular basis and specificity of Beat-Side interactions, we report here the structure of a Beat-Side complex, Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI. The structure shows a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors, especially PD-1-PD-L1, nectins, SynCAMs, Dprs, and DIPs
papers.summary: This study reports the first structure of a Beat-Side protein complex (Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI), revealing a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors like PD-1-PD-L1. The findings demonstrate that this interaction is essential for proper neuromuscular system wiring in Drosophila, providing molecular insights into axon guidance and synaptic connectivity.

papers.title: Selection of Novel DNA Aptamers against HPV16 Biomarker, E6: An In Silico Approach.
papers.abstract: HPV16 E6 oncoprotein is a critical driver of cervical cancer, necessitating high-specificity diagnostic and therapeutic agents. This study presents an in silico selection of DNA aptamers targeting the HPV16 E6 oncoprotein through a pipeline of structure-based virtual screening and 250 ns Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations, performed with GROMACS 2019 and the CHARMM36M. Using AutoDock VinaXB and HADDOCK, eight candidates were evaluated against the F2 RNA benchmark. MM/PBSA calculations identified Apt 9 (-143.76 kcal/mol) as the most potent lead, surpassing the affinity of the F2 control (-142.
papers.summary: This study used computer simulations to design DNA aptamers that bind to the HPV16 E6 oncoprotein, a key driver of cervical cancer. Through virtual screening and molecular dynamics simulations, the researchers identified Apt 9 as a promising candidate with binding affinity comparable to an existing RNA benchmark. This in silico approach could accelerate the development of diagnostic and therapeutic agents for HPV-related cancers.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: Researchers created an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences, enabling high-throughput virtual screening without the need for costly structure prediction. This resource allows scientists to rapidly screen peptide binders and modulators for drug discovery and structural biology applications.

papers.title: Targeting breast cancer with 1,2,4-trioxanes: synthesis, cytotoxicity, and molecular docking insights.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer remains the most common malignancy among women to date, with increasing incidence and resistance to conventional therapies driving the search for novel treatments. 1,2,4-Trioxanes, known for their antimalarial activity, have emerged as promising anticancer agents due to their ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through iron-mediated activation, selectively inducing apoptosis in cancer cells. In this study, a series of hydroxy-functionalized and hemi-succinate trioxane derivatives were synthesized and evaluated against the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line. Among them, c
papers.summary: This study synthesized new 1,2,4-trioxane derivatives and tested them against breast cancer cells, finding that some compounds showed promising anticancer activity by generating reactive oxygen species. The work provides preliminary insights into potential new treatments for drug-resistant breast cancer.

papers.title: Unveiling acetamide coupled benzothiazole hydrazones as potential anti-proliferative agents: an experimental and computational approach.
papers.abstract: A series of benzothiazole acetamide hydrazones (8a-p) was synthesized and evaluated for anti-proliferative activity against various human cancer cell lines, including a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CAPAN-1), a colorectal carcinoma (HCT-116), a leukemia (HL60), a glioblastoma (LN229), a T lymphoblast (Molt-4), a non-small cell lung cancer (NCI-H460), and a B lymphoblast (Z138) cell line. Among the tested derivatives, compounds 8l, 8n, and 8o emerged as lead compounds, exhibiting cytotoxicity across multiple cell lines. Notably, compound 8o demonstrated selective cytotoxicity towards Z138 a
papers.summary: Researchers synthesized a new series of benzothiazole-based compounds and tested them against seven different human cancer cell lines. Three compounds showed strong anti-cancer activity, with one compound (8o) being particularly selective for a B lymphoblast cancer cell line. This work combines experimental synthesis with computational analysis to identify potential new anti-cancer drug candidates.

papers.title: Pharmacophore-based virtual screening, molecular docking, MD simulation, MM-GBSA energy calculation and DFT analysis for the in silico discovery of a SaFtsZ inhibitor.
papers.abstract: The rapid emergence of antimicrobial resistance demands the discovery of new antibacterial targets and inhibitors. Staphylococcus aureus filamenting temperature-sensitive protein (SaFtsZ), an essential cytoskeletal protein involved in bacterial cytokinesis and Z-ring formation, has gained attention as a promising target for antibacterial drug discovery. In the present study, an integrated computational strategy involving pharmacophore mapping, molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and density functional theory (DFT) analysis was employed to identify potential SaFtsZ inhibitor
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to find a potential inhibitor of SaFtsZ, a protein essential for bacterial cell division in Staphylococcus aureus. By combining pharmacophore modeling, molecular docking, and dynamics simulations, the authors identified a promising compound that could serve as a starting point for developing new antibiotics against drug-resistant bacteria.

papers.title: Multiply Perturbed Response: A Computational Protocol to Identify Cooperative Allosteric Residue Combinations Driving Protein Conformational Transitions.
papers.abstract: Protein function often depends on dynamic conformational transitions driven by external factors or molecular interactions. Understanding the allosteric mechanisms underlying these transitions is essential for mechanistic insight into protein function. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are widely used to study protein dynamics; however, capturing large-scale, rare transitions is computationally expensive. To address this, we previously developed Perturbation Response Scanning (PRS), based on elastic network models and linear response theory, but PRS is limited in capturing collective effects
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational protocol called Multiply Perturbed Response (MPR) that extends existing methods to identify combinations of allosteric residues that cooperatively drive protein conformational changes. By using elastic network models and linear response theory, MPR captures collective effects that previous approaches missed, offering a more efficient way to study large-scale protein dynamics without expensive molecular dynamics simulations.

papers.title: Hybrid double-atom catalysts for the carbon monoxide reduction reaction: multiscale coupling of active site electronics and electrolyte microenvironments.
papers.abstract: Hybrid double-atom catalysts (HDACs) provide a versatile platform to steer the carbon monoxide reduction reaction (CORR) toward C2 products. However, the underlying principles connecting atomic-site electronics, electrolyte microenvironments and selectivity remain unclear. This work develops a mechanistic picture for carbon nitride (CN)-supported HDACs composed of 3d-metal and p-block-element dual centers by integrating density functional theory (DFT), ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD), constant-potential simulations, and interpretable machine learning. Screening 36 HDACs identified stable d
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to understand how hybrid double-atom catalysts (HDACs) can improve the conversion of carbon monoxide into valuable C2 products. By combining density functional theory, molecular dynamics, and machine learning, the researchers identified key principles linking the electronic structure of the catalyst and the surrounding electrolyte environment to reaction selectivity. The work provides a mechanistic framework for designing more efficient catalysts for CO reduction.

papers.title: Topological deep learning for drug-target interaction, virtual screening, and docking scoring: a practical, benchmark-driven review.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence is now central to computational drug discovery, yet performance in core tasks-drug-target interaction (DTI) prediction, virtual screening (VS), and docking scoring-is still limited by the multiscale geometric nature of molecular recognition and by evaluation pitfalls such as dataset bias and leakage. Topological deep learning (TDL) offers a complementary route to encode global and multiscale structure from ligands, binding pockets, surfaces, and protein-ligand complexes via persistent homology and related constructions. This review provides a practical, task-driven synt
papers.summary: This review examines how topological deep learning (TDL), which uses persistent homology to capture multiscale geometric features of molecules, can improve drug-target interaction prediction, virtual screening, and docking scoring. The authors highlight that TDL offers a complementary approach to traditional methods by encoding global molecular structure, but caution that evaluation pitfalls like dataset bias remain critical challenges.

papers.title: Low-Temperature xTB-MD-DFT Screening of Functionalized Oxide Surface-Patch Models (TiO2, ZnO, CeO2) for Hydrocarbon Association and Microbial-Proxy Perturbation Assessment in Cold Bioremediation.
papers.abstract: Hydrocarbon biodegradation in cold environments is constrained not only by microbial catabolic capacity but also by interfacial access to poorly soluble substrates and by the way remediation materials interact with microbial envelope-related structures. This study presents an uncertainty-aware low-temperature computational screening workflow for prioritizing functionalized oxide surface-patch models that may favor hydrocarbon association while avoiding excessive perturbation of simplified microbial-interface proxies. Twelve finite oxide-ligand candidates derived from TiO2, ZnO, and CeO2 patche
papers.summary: This study presents a computational screening workflow to identify functionalized oxide surface patches (TiO2, ZnO, CeO2) that enhance hydrocarbon association while minimizing disruption to microbial interfaces, aiming to improve cold bioremediation. The approach uses low-temperature xTB-MD-DFT simulations to prioritize materials that balance substrate access with microbial compatibility.

papers.title: Sustained release of Eugenol from MCM-41 mesoporous silica for preservation of Agaricus bisporus.
papers.abstract: The postharvest quality of Agaricus bisporus declines rapidly during refrigerated storage. A controlled-release system (Eugenol@MCM-41) was prepared by loading eugenol into MCM-41, with a loading capacity of 51%. Physicochemical characterization and all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were combined to clarify the sustained-release mechanism. MD simulations provided supportive evidence for favorable eugenol-silica interfacial interactions and restricted surface-associated mobility in the mesoporous model. In vitro release exhibited clear medium dependence and followed the Weibull model
papers.summary: Researchers developed a controlled-release system (Eugenol@MCM-41) by loading eugenol into mesoporous silica to preserve the postharvest quality of Agaricus bisporus mushrooms during refrigerated storage. Molecular dynamics simulations revealed favorable eugenol-silica interactions and restricted surface mobility that enable sustained release, with in vitro release following the Weibull model.

papers.title: Collective fluctuations underlying nanobody inhibitory activity targeting B. anthracis S-layers revealed by multiscale simulations.
papers.abstract: Nanobodies (Nbs) that depolymerize bacterial surface-layers (S-layers) offer a route to antivirulence therapeutics, but their mechanisms have been difficult to infer from binding structures alone. In B. anthracis, several Nbs bind to Sap, the S-layer protein that assembles into a paracrystalline lattice surrounding the cell. Despite similar binding poses and sequences, only a subset of these ("inhibitory") Nbs induce disassembly of the protein lattice, ultimately abrogating pathogenicity. In this study, we leveraged multiscale simulations to test whether representative Nb-induced fluctuations
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study uses multiscale simulations to investigate why only certain nanobodies inhibit the S-layer of B. anthracis, finding that inhibitory nanobodies induce specific collective fluctuations in the S-layer protein lattice that lead to disassembly. The work provides mechanistic insights into how nanobody binding can disrupt bacterial surface layers, which could inform the design of antivirulence therapeutics.

papers.title: Targeting bromodomain and extraterminal proteins in cardiovascular disease: Pathological mechanisms and therapeutic applications.
papers.abstract: Bromodomain and extraterminal proteins have emerged as key epigenetic regulators that coordinate transcriptional programs fundamental to cardiovascular physiology and disease. Although bromodomain and extraterminal protein inhibitors exert multiple cardiovascular effects, they cannot fully explain clinical heterogeneity in outcomes and responses, highlighting the need to clarify the context- and disease-specific roles of individual bromodomain and extraterminal protein family members. This narrative review delineates the distinct molecular roles of bromodomain-containing protein 2, bromodomain
papers.journal: The Journal of international medical research
papers.summary: This review examines how bromodomain and extraterminal (BET) proteins regulate gene expression in cardiovascular health and disease, and discusses the therapeutic potential of BET inhibitors. The authors highlight that current inhibitors cannot fully explain variable clinical outcomes, emphasizing the need to understand the specific roles of individual BET family members in different disease contexts.

papers.title: OrgNet+: towards robust protein stability prediction with convolutional neural networks.
papers.abstract: Predicting the effect of single-point mutations on protein stability is a central problem in molecular biology and protein engineering. Recent structure-based deep learning methods, particularly 3D convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs), have achieved strong predictive performance by leveraging high-resolution protein structures. However, proteins exist as heterogeneous conformational ensembles rather than single static structures, and the impact of conformational flexibility on structure-based ΔΔG predictors remains poorly characterized. Consequently, current models may yield unstable or eve
papers.summary: This study introduces OrgNet+, a 3D convolutional neural network that improves protein stability prediction by accounting for conformational flexibility, addressing a key limitation of existing structure-based methods that rely on single static structures. The work highlights how incorporating protein dynamics can lead to more robust predictions of mutation effects on stability.

papers.title: seq2ribo: structure-aware integration of machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences.
papers.abstract: Ribosome dynamics are vital in the process of protein expression. Current methods rely on ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq), RNA-seq profiles, and full genomic context. This restricts their use in de novo sequence design, like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines. Simulation-only approaches like the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) oversimplify translation by focusing solely on codon elongation times.
papers.summary: seq2ribo is a new computational method that integrates machine learning with simulation to predict ribosome location profiles directly from RNA sequences, without needing experimental data like Ribo-seq or full genomic context. This enables its use in de novo sequence design, such as for mRNA vaccines, overcoming limitations of current methods that require extensive experimental input or oversimplify translation dynamics.

papers.title: Discovery of a novel STING-binding peptide associated with reduced cGAMP-induced inflammatory gene expression.
papers.abstract: cGAMP-induced STING activation contributes to inflammatory and interferon-related signalling, making STING a relevant target for inhibitor development. In this study, a 59,319-sequence peptide library was screened against STING by molecular docking, and four top-ranked peptides were selected for evaluation. MST analysis demonstrated that Peptides 1-4 bound to recombinant STING, with Peptide-1 showing the highest affinity (Kd = 0.15 ± 0.01 μM). Docking and simulation analyses suggested that binding was mediated by hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic contacts. Molecular dynamics, MM/PBSA, and free
papers.journal: Journal of enzyme inhibition and medicinal chemistry
papers.summary: Researchers identified a novel peptide that binds to the STING protein with high affinity, potentially blocking its activation and reducing inflammation. Using computational screening of a large peptide library, they found Peptide-1 as the strongest binder, which could lead to new anti-inflammatory therapies.

papers.title: Integrative genomic and molecular dynamics characterisation of acrB_R717L/Q mutations in azithromycin-resistant Salmonella Typhi from India.
papers.abstract: Azithromycin remains a highly effective oral therapy for uncomplicated typhoid fever caused by extensively drug-resistant (XDR) Salmonella Typhi strains. However, azithromycin resistance has been reported in low and middle-income countries (LMICs). In this study, we identified three azithromycin-resistant S. Typhi isolates and performed phylogenetic analysis along with molecular dynamics simulation to assess the structural stability and conformational dynamics of the acrB efflux pump harbouring R717L/Q mutations.
papers.summary: This study identifies three azithromycin-resistant Salmonella Typhi isolates from India and uses genomic analysis and molecular dynamics simulations to characterize how R717L/Q mutations in the acrB efflux pump contribute to resistance. The findings provide insight into the structural basis of azithromycin resistance in typhoid, which is concerning for treatment of extensively drug-resistant strains.

papers.title: Integrative computational-experimental discovery and translation of antifungal peptides for multidrug-resistant fungi.
papers.abstract: Multidrug-resistant fungal infections caused by Candida and Aspergillus species have become one of the major global health concerns, especially among immunocompromised individuals. The small number of antifungals available and the rapid emergence of resistance to azoles, echinocandins and polyenes underscore the urgent need to develop alternative therapeutic strategies with different mechanisms of action. Antifungal peptides (AFPs) have attracted increasing attention as promising candidates due to their broad-spectrum activity, multimodal mechanisms of action, and their low likelihood of resis
papers.summary: This study uses an integrative computational-experimental approach to discover and validate antifungal peptides (AFPs) effective against multidrug-resistant Candida and Aspergillus species. The authors demonstrate that these AFPs have broad-spectrum activity and multimodal mechanisms, making them promising candidates for treating resistant fungal infections.

papers.title: Immunoinformatics-driven design of a multi-epitope vaccine against Clostridium perfringens in yaks.
papers.abstract: Clostridium perfringens is the primary causative agent of enterotoxemia in yaks, resulting in substantial economic losses on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau. Conventional vaccines exhibit limited protective breadth and suboptimal efficacy, highlighting the need for innovative strategies. Here, we aimed to construct a novel vaccine candidate incorporating epitopes from multiple prevalent toxinotypes (A, C, E) of C. perfringens affecting yaks, using immunoinformatics approach.
papers.summary: This study uses computational immunoinformatics to design a multi-epitope vaccine targeting three toxinotypes of Clostridium perfringens that cause enterotoxemia in yaks. The approach aims to overcome the limited protection of conventional vaccines by incorporating epitopes from multiple prevalent toxinotypes. This represents a computational design strategy that could potentially improve vaccine efficacy for livestock on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau.

papers.title: An integrative coding and non-coding SNPs analysis of the CLDN-3 gene in humans to identify high-priority variants using in-silico analysis.
papers.abstract: By creating intramembrane tight-junction barriers, claudin-3 (CLDN-3), a vital member of the claudin protein family, helps to preserve cell polarity. Through aberrant signaling pathways, the dysregulation of CLDN-3 has been linked to gynecological and other epithelial cancers. While previous studies have explored its role in cancer progression, the functional impact of both coding and non-coding single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in CLDN-3 remains largely unexplored.
papers.summary: This study computationally analyzed coding and non-coding genetic variants in the CLDN-3 gene to identify those most likely to impact protein function and disease risk. The authors prioritized several high-impact SNPs that may affect tight junction integrity and contribute to gynecological and epithelial cancers. This work provides a list of candidate variants for further experimental validation in cancer research.

papers.title: Repurposing natural compounds: computational evaluation of Piper longum alkaloids against ovarian cancer transcriptomic signatures.
papers.abstract: Ovarian cancer is a deadly gynaecological cancer, diagnosed at advanced stages, resistant to chemotherapy and with low survival rates. Current treatments are ineffective, and new multi-targeted candidates are needed. The alkaloids of Piper longum (piperine, piperlongumine, and piperlonguminine) have reported anti-cancer effects however, specific molecular targets and underlying mechanism of action in ovarian cancer remain largely unknown.
papers.summary: This study computationally evaluated three alkaloids from Piper longum (piperine, piperlongumine, and piperlonguminine) against ovarian cancer transcriptomic signatures, identifying potential multi-targeted mechanisms of action. The findings suggest these natural compounds may be promising candidates for further development as ovarian cancer therapeutics.

papers.title: Chemical multiplexing in the nervous system: molecular architecture, functional stratification, and pathophysiological plasticity of neuropeptide-classical neurotransmitter cotransmission.
papers.abstract: Neurons frequently synthesize both neuropeptides and classical low-molecular-weight neurotransmitters, challenging the historical interpretation of one-neuron-one-transmitter signaling. Rather than representing biochemical redundancy, coexistence reflects a conserved organizational strategy that expands neural coding capacity through chemical multiplexing. Dual vesicular architecture-small clear vesicles for rapid synaptic transmission and large dense-core vesicles for activity-dependent peptide release-creates stimulus-intensity-dependent recruitment of distinct signaling layers. Classical tr
papers.summary: This review challenges the historical 'one-neuron-one-transmitter' dogma by highlighting that neurons commonly co-release neuropeptides and classical neurotransmitters, a phenomenon termed 'chemical multiplexing.' The authors describe how distinct vesicle types enable stimulus-dependent release of different signaling molecules, expanding neural coding capacity. They also discuss how this system undergoes plasticity in disease states, offering new insights into brain function and potential therapeutic targets.

papers.title: Computational evaluation of selected compounds as potential inhibitors of gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase using molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA, and ADMET analyses.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the inhibitory potential of selected phytochemicals and conventional drugs against gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, an important therapeutic target associated with acid-related gastrointestinal disorders such as peptic ulcers and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Computational approaches, including molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA free energy calculations, and ADMET profiling, were employed to evaluate the pharmacological relevance of the selected compounds. Molecular docking analysis revealed that several compounds exhibited favorable interactions with the
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to evaluate how well certain plant compounds and existing drugs might inhibit gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, a key target for treating acid-related stomach disorders. Molecular docking showed some compounds had promising interactions, but the study is purely computational and lacks experimental validation.

papers.title: Rational design 2.0: transitioning from static structural biology to computational prioritization and iterative vaccine optimization for RSV.
papers.abstract: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a major cause of severe lower respiratory tract disease (LRTD) in infants and older adults worldwide. Although vaccines based on the fusion (F) protein have shown progress, their efficacy remains limited by antigenic instability and viral evolution. The metastable transition of the F protein between prefusion (preF) and postF conformations critically determines the exposure of neutralizing epitopes, with most potent antibodies targeting preF-specific sites. In addition, the glycosylated G protein contributes to immune evasion through glycan shielding and CX
papers.summary: This review discusses how computational methods and structural biology are being combined to design better RSV vaccines, focusing on stabilizing the prefusion form of the fusion protein to improve immune responses. The authors propose a 'Rational Design 2.0' approach that uses computational prioritization and iterative optimization to overcome challenges like antigen instability and viral evolution.

papers.title: Structural homology of HHV-6B epitopes as candidates for molecular mimicry triggers of the onset type one diabetes mellitus.
papers.abstract: To study whether HHV-6B can act as a molecular mimic to trigger onset of Type One Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) by assessing structural and binding similarities between HHV-6B derived epitopes and T1DM autoantigen derived epitopes.
papers.summary: This study investigates whether human herpesvirus 6B (HHV-6B) can trigger type 1 diabetes through molecular mimicry, by comparing the structural and binding similarities between viral epitopes and diabetes-related autoantigen epitopes. The findings suggest that certain HHV-6B epitopes share structural homology with T1DM autoantigens, supporting a potential role for viral infection in disease onset.

papers.title: Convergent GenX biodegradation by genomically designed and functionally screened synthetic bacterial consortia.
papers.abstract: GenX (hexafluoropropylene oxide dimer acid) has been introduced as a replacement for legacy polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) compounds; however, accumulating evidence indicates this C6 ether-PFAS may exhibit comparable toxicity to PFOA while persisting in contaminated water supplies. Biological remediation offers genuine mineralization potential but has been constrained by the exceptional stability of carbon-fluorine bonds. Here, we demonstrate that synthetic bacterial consortia assembled through rational genomic design (GENIA) or microfluidic selection (Community G) achieve rapid GenX biode
papers.summary: Researchers developed synthetic bacterial consortia, either through rational genomic design or microfluidic selection, that can rapidly biodegrade GenX, a replacement PFAS compound with potential toxicity similar to PFOA. This approach overcomes the challenge of breaking stable carbon-fluorine bonds, offering a promising biological remediation strategy for contaminated water supplies.

papers.title: Liposomal Nanoparticulate Drug Delivery Systems: Strategies to Destabilize Biological Membranes at the Target Tissue.
papers.abstract: Liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs) are clinically validated nanomedicine platforms seeing regular use in oncology and infectious disease. Their applications have rapidly expanded with several tissue targeting formulations in early-phase clinical trials. Beyond small molecular drugs, LNDDSs are increasingly employed for delivery of nucleic acid therapeutics, such as ribonucleic acid (RNA) based vaccines and immunomodulators. Recent advances in nanomaterials have enabled LNDDSs not only to transport therapeutic agents across systemic biological barriers but also to selectiv
papers.summary: This review discusses liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs), which are clinically validated platforms used in oncology and infectious disease. It highlights their expanding applications, including targeting specific tissues and delivering nucleic acid therapeutics like RNA vaccines, enabled by recent advances in nanomaterials.
